original_abstract: Electrocatalysis provides a transformative pathway for the localized, on-demand production of industrial chemicals using intermittent renewable energy, enhancing grid resilience while streamlining supply logistics. In this work, we demonstrate the efficacy of a molecular copper boron-imidazolate cage, BIF-29(Cu), as a catalyst for the co-reduction of CO₂ and NO₃⁻ to produce urea. This system achieves a high selectivity of 68.5% and a current density of 424 μA cm⁻², establishing BIF-29(Cu) as one of the most selective catalysts identified to date for this complex C–N coupling reaction. Notably, this performance is achieved despite the catalyst possessing isolated, single-metal active sites. Utilizing a combination of electrochemical measurements, in situ spectroscopy, and atomic-scale simulations, we investigated the underlying Reaction mechanism. Our findings reveal that CO₂ and NO₃⁻ reduction reactions occur in tandem at distinct copper sites, with the C–N bond formation proceeding via the condensation of *CO and NH₂OH intermediates. This study underscores the potential of supramolecular metal–organic cages with precision-engineered, discrete active sites to facilitate highly efficient multi-reactant coupling processes.
humanized_abstract: We report the use of the molecular copper boron-imidazolate cage catalyst BIF-29(Cu) for the electroco-reduction of CO2 and NO3– into urea. We observe an exceptionally high selectivity of 68.5% at a current density of 424 μA cm–2 with BIF-29(Cu) being ranked amongst the most selective catalystes to ever be reported for this reaction. Remarkably, BIF-29(Cu) contains isolated, single-metal active sites. By combining electrochemical measurements with in situ mass spectrometry and ex-situ spectroscopies along with atomic-scale simulations, we elucidate the reaction mechanism. Our data reveals the parallel operation of the CO2 and NO3– reduction reactions on the copper sites of BIF-29(Cu) with the C–N bond formation occurring by the condensation of *CO with *NH2OH. Our results therefore demonstrate that supramolecular metal–organic cages, where specific discrete active sites can be designed, can facilitate multi-reactant complex coupling reactions with high efficiencies.

Our data reveals the parallel operation of the CO2 and NO3– reduction reactions on the copper sites of BIF-29(Cu) with the C–N bond formation occurring by the condensation of *CO with *NH2OH. Our results therefore demonstrate that supramolecular metal–organic cages, where specific discrete active sites can be designed, can facilitate multi-reactant complex coupling reactions with high efficiencies.
